condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative:

Number of patents and trademarks filed 5. Number of Science, Technology, Engineering, Agriculture, and Mathematics (STEAM) SEs incubated by university partners 6. Percent of ISIP certified professors 7. Number of women among ISIP certified professors 8. No. of ISIP partner HEIs that integrate the SE policy agenda in their “strategic plans” 9. No. of analytical and research work produced by ISIP
Based Revised Indicator (2019) 1. Number of Social Enterprise (SE) benficiaries (by sex, Social Enterprise (SE) type) 2. No of Social Enterprise (Social Enterprise (SE)s) with an improved performance score in Business Operations by 10% 3. "Number of Social Enterprise (Social Enterprise (SE)s) utilizing internal M&E information systems to track social impact 4. Number of Social Enterprise (SE) beneficiaries (by sex, Social Enterprise (SE) type) that are capital-ready 5. "Number of Social Enterprise (SE) beneficiaries (by sex, Social Enterprise (SE) type) with streamlined financial systems/ documents 6. Number of Higher Education Institution (HEI) beneficiaries with Visiting Professor initiatives 7. Number of trained HEIs that develop Social Entrepreneurship integration plans 8. Number of trained faculty members with improved knowledge on incorporating Social Entrepreneurship in Teaching programs (by sex, by type) 9. Number of HEI incubators trained to integrate Social Enterprise (SE) support in their Business Operation 10. Number of trained Technology Business Incubator managers with improved knowledge on incubation facility management 11. Number of Social Enterprise (SE) stakeholders that download the Social Enterprise (SE) knowledge products developed by ISIP (by sex, stakeholder/product type) 12. Number of HEIs that received Social Enterprise (SE) Governance Reco

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
1.) Indicator 1: GEF Core Indicator 4: Area of landscapes under improved practices (excluding Protected areas), including: - Area of landscapes under improved management to benefit biodiversity; 2.) Indicator 2: GEF Core Indicator 6: Greenhouse gas emissions mitigated (metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent) as measured by: - Carbon sequestrated or emissions avoided in the sector of agriculture, forestry and other land uses 3.) Indicator 3: GEF Core Indicator 11: Number of direct beneficiaries disaggregated by gender as co-benefits of GEF investment 4.) Indicator 4: Number of policy instruments that are in place and applied to integrate biodiversity outcomes in sector and national and local planning policy and programs 5.) Indicator 5: Level of institutional capacities for planning, implementation and monitoring integrated biodiversity management planning in biodiversity corridors as measured by UNDP’s capacity development scorecard for the following institutions:
1. Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) 2. Department of Agriculture (DA) 3. National Commission for Indigenous People (NCIP)
6.) Indicator 6: Extent to which the network of protected areas and other Effective Area-based Conservation Measures (OECMs) within corridors have adopted automated biodiversity monitoring system for biodiversity and threat assessment Baseline Targets / Cumulative Results End of Project Target (2027) 7.) Indicator 7 (GEF Core Indicator 1.2): Management effectiveness of terrestrial protected areas within designated biological corridors supported by tenure security and improved resource access and sustainable use 8.) Indicator 8: Status of status of key species remaining stable or increasing from the baseline: Central Mindoro: Forest obligate species such as Tamaraw (Bubalus mindorensis ); and Mindoro Bleeding heart pigeon (Gallicolumba platanae) Eastern Mindanao: Forest obligate species such as Mindanao Bleeding heart pigeon (Gallicolumba crinigera) and Phili

comment.narrative: 1.) Around 4-7% of forests within biodiversity hot-spots in selected clusters under threat of further fragmentation 2.) Limited efforts within project biodiversity corridors to assess carbon values 3.) Baseline of households participating in improved and alternative livelihoods and sustainable resource management will be established through the community cluster conservation planning process in Year 1 4.) Current policies are limited to detection of presence or absence of listed species rather than looking at impacts on broader ecological principles and processes for the survival of species, maintenance of ecological services, and habitat connectivity. 5.) Limited institutional capacities for planning, implementation and monitoring of multiple use IBM planning and management in biological corridors as measured by UNDP Capacity 6.) Monitoring system in protected areas paper-based and inefficient to capture real-time monitoring of biodiversity and threats. No monitoring system exists in OECMs 7.) Baseline METT scores20: PA 1: Mts. Iglit Baco NP: 67 PA 2: Mt. Calavite WS: 67 PA 3: Agusan Marsh WS: 55 PA 4: Alamio, Buayan, Caracan, Panikian River and Sipangpang Falls Watershed FR: 15 PA 5: Aliwagwag Protected Landscape: 26 PA 6: Andanan Watershed FR: 30 PA 7: Cabadbaran Watershed: 20 PA 8: Mainit Hotspring Protected Landscape: 42 8.) Key species under continued threat from forest loss and degradation and poaching. Limited attention and prioritization of SLM and SFM activities in RDIPs, PPFPs and LGU CLUPs 10.) Limited attention and prioritization of SLM and SFM activities in RDIPs, PPFPs and LGU CLUPs 11.) description.narrative: Extent to which the network of protected areas and other Effective Area-based Conservation Measures (OECMs) within corridors have adopted automated biodiversity monitoring system for biodiversity and threat assessment

comment.narrative: Monitoring system in protected areas paper-based and inefficient to capture real-time monitoring of biodiversity and threats. No monitoring system exists in OECMs
All protected areas (11) and OECMs (at least 9 ICCAs and 4 LCAs) within two biodiversity corridors have moved to automated system of monitoring of biodiversity and threats
Enhanced Socio-economic Assessment and Monitoring System (eSEAMS) has been pilot tested in 3 PAs (MIBNP and MCWS in MBC and APL in EMBC)

description.narrative: Status of status of key species remaining stable or increasing from the baseline

comment.narrative: Key species under continued threat from forest loss and degradation and poaching. Baselines23 are: Mindoro: Tamaraw + 500 (DENR 2018): Mindoro Bleeding Heart pigeon -50- 249 mature adults (Birdlife 2018) Mindanao Mindanao Bleeding Heart Pigeon -1,000- 2,499 mature adults (Birdlife 2018); Philippine Eagle -180-500 mature adults (Birdlife 2018)
Key species populations stable or increasing from baseline values

Monitoring protocols for the Tamaraw and Philippine Eagle has been established;
Monitoring protocol for the Mindoro Bleeding heart pigeon and Mindanao Bleeding heart pigeon developed and pilot tested in the two biodiversity corridors

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

comment.narrative: Rules and regulations for ABS exist but are uncoordinated, inconsistent, and not followed
Bioprospecting guidelines and relevant supporting rules and regulations adopted

Gender experts from Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) and the Palawan Council for Sustainable Development (PCSD) were involved in the policy review of the revised and harmonized guidelines on Bioprospecting. Women community leaders were explicitly targeted in the capacity-building and awareness-raising, including the consultation process. Gender consideration was included in the draft guidelines.

title.narrative: NIP updated and endorsed by government, with an updated inventory of POPs and new POPs listed after 2013

description.narrative: NIP updated and endorsed by government, with an updated inventory of POPs and new POPs listed after 2013

comment.narrative: NIP update published in 2014 with 2012 data for inventory of POPs. Inventory of new POPs listed by SC after 2013 are not available.

A NIP technical team is established, and a plan for the NIP update established and implemented.
A first draft of the NIP is completed and submitted to the GoP for approval
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Availability of updated inventories of POPs and new POPs

description.narrative: Availability of updated inventories of POPs and new POPs

comment.narrative:
Inventory of new POPs listed by SC after 2013 are not available.
Inventories of POPs with 2012 data need to be updated.

An inventory team is established for each class of POPs: pesticides, industrial, U-POPs, PCBs.
Preliminary inventories of all POPs are completed
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of manufacturing sectors and associated priorities identified and assessed for GC implementation

description.narrative: Number of manufacturing sectors and associated priorities identified and assessed for GC implementation

comment.narrative: A preliminary assessment of a number of manufacturing sectors was developed by BOI and DTI in 2015
Manufacturing sectors identified and confirmed, starting from the ones proposed at PIF and already assessed by BOI (at least 6). The survey on at least 30 factories completed.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Indicator 7: Availability of a roadmap for greening the selected manufacturing sectors.

description.narrative: Indicator 7: Availability of a roadmap for greening the selected manufacturing sectors.

comment.narrative: A Greening industry roadmap roadmap has been developed by BOI and DTI for a number of manufacturing sector in 2015 but it was not implemented and need to be updated.

A bilateral national team (DTI/BOI and EMB/DENR) will be established to develop the roadmap in the selected manufacturing sectors.
Roadmaps for all the selected manufacturing sectors developed and endorsed by the GoP and the stakeholders.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Green financing mechanism is fully designed and established

description.narrative: Green financing mechanism is fully designed and established

comment.narrative: Some incentive mechanisms supporting industries to implement green process are in place (Tax incentives for green jobs; Philippine Environmental Partnership Programme.) Coordination with these incentives to be established
Green financing mechanism designed, and approved, including eligibility criteria, replenishment modality, and coordination with environmental incentives already in place.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: The green financing mechanism with a fund size of USD15,000,000.00 is established and operational

description.narrative: The green financing mechanism with a fund size of USD15,000,000.00 is established and operational

comment.narrative: A green financing mechanism to support Green Chemistry implementation and the reduction of POPs is currently missing.

A dedicated office to manage the FREEME financing mechanism is established.
FREEME funded with a first budget of at least USD5,000,000.00.
Applicants submit and receive approval for eligible projects for at least 50 % of the initial budget.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Amount of POPs directly avoided or prevented through the implementation of Green Chemistry in pilot factories.

description.narrative: Amount of POPs directly avoided or prevented through the implementation of Green Chemistry in pilot factories.

comment.narrative:
Limited knowledge and awareness about presence of POPs in the manufacturing sectors.
Green chemistry approach mostly unknown in the Philippine.

4 industries from the key manufacturing sectors identified and supported in their application to the FREEME.
Implementation of GC started in all the selected factories.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of technical guidance documents developed for the implementation of Green Chemistry

description.narrative: Number of technical guidance documents developed for the implementation of Green Chemistry

comment.narrative: Technical guidance for the implementation of Green Chemistry in manufacturing industry are not localized for the Philippines.
Technical guidance developed for the implementation of Green Chemistry developed for at least 2 sectors based on the practical experience achieved in the pilot plants.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Availability of new/amended regulations to ban import of the POPS listed by SC after 2013.

description.narrative: Availability of new/amended regulations to ban import of the POPS listed by SC after 2013.

comment.narrative: The current regulatory framework does not include yet rules on new POPS listed by SC after 2013.
Draft of the regulation to ban the import and use of new POPs in manufacturing industry, as well as limits for POPs in products, is prepared and submitted to the GoP for approval
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of factories where PRTR is demonstrated

description.narrative: Number of factories where PRTR is demonstrated

comment.narrative:
No factories yet
In 2017 House Bill No. 6225 an Act Creating a Philippine Pollutant Release and Transfer Registry was introduced during the 17th Congress.
However, the PRTR was not implemented
The PRTR system (including list of regulated pollutants and list of regulated activities) drafted and submitted for approval to the GoP. A PRTR software developed.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of women and men custom officers trained on POPs

description.narrative: Number of women and men custom officers trained on POPs

comment.narrative: In many situations where the customs authorities have not the knowledge or the capacity to identify POPs, products containing POPs may still be legally imported because POPs are not clearly specified in the HS code list.

HS code list amended.
Training of customs control officers at major international harbors (at least 10 officers trained)
Technical manuals for the identification of POPs in imported articles, mixtures and pesticides developed.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Availability of HS codes for new POPs developed and endorsed

description.narrative: Availability of HS codes for new POPs developed and endorsed

comment.narrative: In many situations where the customs authorities have not the knowledge or the capacity to identify POPs, products containing POPs may still be legally imported because POPs are not clearly specified in the HS code list

HS code list amended.
Technical manuals for the identification of POPs in imported articles, mixtures and pesticides developed.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of project staff appointed (F/M)

description.narrative: Number of project staff appointed (F/M)

comment.narrative: All the project staff required for the PMU and the PSC is appointed within 3 months from project start, with a proportion F/M not smaller than 1 and with at least one female staff in apical position.
No progress yet as of this date.

title.narrative: Number of lessons and best practices learn and shared by the project management team.

description.narrative: Number of lessons and best practices learn and shared by the project management team.

comment.narrative:
Procedures for the acquisition and exchange of information and knowledge generated by the project established.
At least one knowledge sharing workshop involving UN/GEF projects on ecolabeling, green chemistry and green financing held where lesson learnt for each project component are shared.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: 1. Number of comprehensive M&E System for Normalization Program (was cancelled by end 2019 and reprogrammed to JPST Station/Barracks Construction); Indicator is now "Number of JPST facilities constructed or renovated" 2. Number of Information system for entire Normalization Programme established, including subsystems and modules corresponding to components of Normalization Programme (was cancelled by end 2019 and reprogrammed to operational support for decommissioning-related activities); Indicator is now "Extent of operational support for Phase 2 Decommissioning activities" (Inadequate - insufficient support leading to non-achievement of Phase 2 Decom-related activities; adequate - sufficient support to enable conduct of Phase 2 decommissioning-related activities; substantial - more than sufficient support to ensure smooth conduct of activities 3. Number. Baseline Studies conducted in 5 control and 5 treatment areas, as agreed upon by GPH and MILF 4. No. of JNC/JPSC meetings, joint advocacy activities, assessments, security sector workshops, communication materials) 5. Number of JPST Training batches completed 8. Number of JPSTs Teams deployed and functioning 9. Number of organizations of women, youth, and vulnerable sectors participating in consultation and planning processes 14. Number of Comprehensive and Integrated Camps Transformation Plan

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
2019 indicators 1. Number of new models, approaches and insights from lab network documented and disseminating through working out loud 2. Number of frontier challenges identified and initiated
2020 revised indicators 1.1 Number of local solutions addressing key local challenges identified by the labs using new methods? 1.2 Number of experiments designed and undertaken by the ALab-PH? 2.1 Number of lead innovators (surfaced through solutions mapping) whose ideas that either a) get taken up by government or b) spin off into stand alone ventures or c) influence UNDP programming? 2.2 Number of solutions surfaced through collective intelligence methods that either a) get taken by government or b) spin off into standalone ventures or c) influence UNDP programming? 3.1 New models approaches, and insights from ALab-PH documented and disseminated through working out loud? 3.1.1 Social media engagement? 3.1.2 Short monthly blogs? 3.1.3 in-depth articles? 3.2 Methods for exploration, solutions mapping, collective intelligence, and experimentation developed, codified, and disseminated? 3.3 Number of regular engagements for exchange of knowledge with other country labs and within PH initiated? 3.3.1 In-country office? 3.3.2 With other country labs? 3.4 Extent to which that the Country Programme was supported through the frontier issues and solutions explored, mapped, and tested by the ALab-PH
2021 Revised indicators 1.1 Methods for exploration, solutions mapping, collective intelligence, and experimentation developed, codified, and disseminated 1.2 Number of activities conducted with Programme and Operations applying methods for exploration, solutions mapping, and experimentation to Country Programme (new) 1.3 Extent to which that the Country Programme was supported through the frontier issues and other challenges where solutions were explored, mapped, and tested with PH Alab 2.1 Number of local solutions addressing key local challenges identified by ALab PH using new method

comment.narrative: 1.1. 3? local solutions addressing key local challenges identified by the labs using new methods? 1.2. 3? experiments designed and undertaken by the ALab-PH? 2.1. 1? lead innovator (surfaced through solutions mapping) whose ideas that either a) get taken up by government or b) spin off into stand alone ventures or c) influence UNDP programming? 2.2. 1? solution surfaced through collective intelligence methods that either a) get taken by government or b) spin off into standalone ventures or c) influence UNDP programming? 3.1.1. significant? social media engagement means ALAB creates 20 social media posts with a target reach of 5,000 engagements or more; non-existent means 0 social media posts and 0 social media engagement 3.1.2. 9? Short monthly blogs? posted 3.1.3. 2? in-depth articles? posted 3.2. 1 Method for exploration, solutions mapping, collective intelligence, and experimentation developed, codified, and disseminated? 3.3.1. 3? regular engagements for exchange of knowledge with PH initiated? (CO) 3.3.2. 3? regular engagements for exchange of knowledge with other country labs 3.4. significant means ALAB provides technical support to programme teams in 3 or more programmatic areas; non-existent means no technical support provided to any programmatic areas

1.1. 5 local solutions addressing key local challenges identified by the lab using new methods? which are the establishment of the ZEP local convergence, advocacy on use of SalikLakbay, promotion of circular economy and digital finance to next generations cities, use of chatbots for data innovation and innovation in the supply chain through EMPOWERPH 1.2. 5 experiments designed and undertaken by the ALab-PH? which were on digital finance (ADEPT - FMML and cash disbursement), marine litter (multi-stakeholder consultation - NPOA), use of chatbots for data collection in surveys, use of satellite imagery for poverty estimation and machine-estimated data on multidimensional poverty and establishment of Pintig Lab for data innovation and analysis. 2.1. 1? lead innovator which is Department of Science and Technology (DOST) wherein their adoption of SalikLakbay was institutionalized in their organization 2.2. 4 solution surfaced through collective intelligence. These were EmpowerPH on supply chain (connects suppliers to beneficiaries), ADEPT for digital financing, MSME chatbot survey, ZEP chatbot survey (promoting local convergence 3.1.1. Significant? – ALab-PH was able to share 32 unique posts under UNDP CO platforms. In Facebook, ALab-PH posts had 96,804 reach, 110,158 impressions, and 7,035 engagements 3.1.2. 4 blogs posted 3.1.3. 2? in-depth articles published/posted 3.2. 1 method of solutions mapping developed, codified, and disseminated? which is the SalikLakbay field guide 3.3.1. 44 meetings/workshops attended within the CO 3.3.2. 16? meetings/workshops attended and participated with other country labs 3.4. Significant - ALab-PH was able to provide technical support to programme teams in facilitating achievement of results particularly in the areas of data innovation, digital cash transfers, inclusive innovation policy research, marine litter and next generation governance.

description.narrative: 1.1.1 Number of MILF communities with local economic development plans following inventory of natural resources and other productive assets 1.2.1 Number of social enterprises or cooperatives organized/reconstituted 1.3.1 Number of individuals trained on the principles and application of sustainable livelihood management, natural resource protection and conservation, and climate change adaptation, among others

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Number of social enterprises or cooperatives organized/reconstituted

description.narrative: Social enteprises/Coopraties are local groups, cooperatives or associations organized and reconstituted with provision of organizational support and livelihood assistance. This shall include completion of registration records, development/updating of operational policies and procedures, and coaching on organizational management and operations assessment.

title.narrative: Number of training design/program finalized with modules on sustainable livelihood management, natural resource protection and conservation, and climate change adaptation

description.narrative: Number of training design/program finalized with modules on sustainable livelihood management, natural resource protection and conservation, and climate change adaptation

title.narrative: Number of individuals trained on the principles and application of sustainable livelihood management, natural resource protection and conservation, and climate change adaptation, among others

description.narrative: Individuals from MILF ( former combatants or their immediate family members who are unemployed or whose household income is considered among the lowest in the community) that are provided with trainings on the contextualised modules onsustainable livelihood management, natural resource protection and conservation, and climate change adaptation

title.narrative: Number of men and women provided with individual non-agricultural (entrepreneurial or vocational) livelihood assistance package

description.narrative: Men and women from the identified communities and camps have access livelihood (both agricultual and non-agricultural. 'Vocational training support, including final assessment/certification, will be delivered by TESDA-BARMM accredited trainers to Decommissioned combatants and their next of kin.

title.narrative: Number of community enterprises assisted to formally enter into "production-and-supply" agreements

description.narrative: Community enterprises are organized and/or exisiting that will be supported to scale-up their livelihood operations through estabilisng partnership agreements with identified business/private secors inside and outside their communities.

title.narrative: Number of MILF communities in the major camps assisted on community needs assessment and baseline data collection

description.narrative: Identlfied MILF Communities completed community needs assessent with completed and validated reports.

title.narrative: Number of LSFs (basic service delivery and project management related) assisted in their organizing, reconstituting and capacity building needs to deliver their mandates

description.narrative: Local social formations (LSF) are organised or exisiting groupd like barangay water and sanitation association, education committee, nutrition committee, technical working group and composite project monitoring committee that will be assisted and supported.

title.narrative: Number of WASH, nutrition, social and spiritual development projects identified, implemented, monitored and completed with community participation

description.narrative: These are identified small-scale community infrastructure projects from CTP that are validated and approved through community consultations and approval of the peace mechanisms.

title.narrative: Number of MILF camp / command leadership supported to lead the camp transformation and community development efforts

description.narrative: Number of MILF camp / command leadership supported to lead the camp transformation and community development efforts

title.narrative: Number of LSFs (on social cohesion and community resilience) assisted in their organizing, reconstituting and capacity building needs to deliver their mandates

description.narrative: Intended to put in place or strengthen local capacities to promote community development, partnership building, peacebuilding, culture of peace, and conflict transformation. LSFs to include local mediation groups, council of elders, council for rido settlement, and intra-faith/women/youth groups. Training and accompaniment support shall be provided by subject matter experts.

title.narrative: Number of LSF priority initiatives fully implemented

description.narrative: These are initiatives of LSFs to deliver their mandates based on the identified issues in the community. Sample activities are mediation and negotiation to settle local disputes, rido settlement, khutbah development, community-led campaign against violent extremism, and IEC campaign on the transition extension and normalization.

title.narrative: Number of community orientations and planning sessions facilitated across the 6 major camps

description.narrative: Minimum of at least 6 consultations per camp per camps, Orientation and planning sessions are essential so that community members are adequately involved in the designing and decision making processes. These sessions will provide ample opportunity for stakeholders to level off on expectations, agree on design/scope of community infrastructure projects, jointly develop reporting and feedback mechanisms, and agree on counterpart contributions and sustainability measures.

condition.narrative: Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

title.narrative: Annual Output Results
Output Indicator

description.narrative:
Outpu 1: E-services and Digital Centres established in pilot BARMM LGUs and Ministries and made accessible to women, senior citizens and PWDs
1.1 Number of core ICT infrastructure (servers and data with software systems) for e-Governance established 1.2 Number of project trained women with increased ICT capacities focused on management of Digital Centres and Interactive Call Centres 1.3 Pool of trainers from LGUs, Ministries, CSOs for Empathy Training and Digital Service Design Lab established with at least 50 percent women trainers 1.4 Number of individuals using Digital Centres and #DigitalBangsamoro portal (disaggregated by gender and marginalized groups - PWD, SCs) 1.5 Number of LGUs and BARMM ministry with updated Citizen’s Charter (e-services integrated)
Output 2: BARMM local government units are able to collect gender-disaggregated household and sectoral data for use in local development and resilience planning.
2.1 Number of LGUs with increased capacities on DevLIVE+ data collection, management, and analysis, including gender- sensitive data collection and analysis 2.2 Percentage of population of targeted barangays in BARMM are recorded in the DevLIVE+ platform disaggregated by sex 2.3 Number of government officials with increased understanding on evidence-based planning
Output 3: The 5-year Digital Transformation Roadmap for Bangsamoro is established with sustainability measures
3.1 Digital Transformation Roadmap published and endorsed by the MILG 3.2 Number of discussion papers on Digital Transformation taken up by the BARMM government 3.3 Number of institutions, local and international, private, and public sectors included in a formal coalition to establish Digital Bangsamoro 3.4 Number of developed operational modules and manuals
Output 4: Program managed efficiently 4.1 Regular meetings of Project Board convened with agreements acted in a timely manner 4.2 Percentage of quarterly targets achieved

title.narrative: WPS in BARMM

description.narrative: Building on the results of an earlier initiative to enhance women’s roles in building community resilience and in the further implementation of the Bangsamoro peace process, this Project will aim to contribute to advancing women’s roles in conflict-prevention, peacebuilding and governance in the Bangsamoro through the following initiatives: (1) assessment and updating of the Bangsamoro Regional Action Plan on Women, Peace and Security; (2) accompaniment of the MILF Bangsamoro Islamic Women Auxil

title.narrative: Activity web page
Enhancing Food Security and Livelihood in Bangsamoro

description.narrative: The project aims to support and sustain peacebuilding and recovery in Bangsamoro on a food supply crisis and food price inflation and the delay of livelihood support under the Peace Process through the following two approaches. The first is short-term assistance in providing immediate food distribution among the worst impacted or more marginalized communities in the BARMM and adjacent areas in Mindanao. The second is mid-term support to improve livelihood in Bangsamoro through enhancing the capacity of Community Cooperative Groups (including women’s associations) and strengthening food value chains for the agricultural and fishery sectors towards food security. The project will also contribute to addressing the socio-economic impacts of natural and human-induced disasters in the BARMM, particularly in the aftermath of tropical storm Nalgae (Paeng) which severely affected vulnerable communities in the region.By improving basic food sufficiency and economic stability in post-conflict Bangsamoro, this project co

title.narrative: Devlive+ in Talibon using CBMS Questions

description.narrative: The Development through Local Indicators and Vulnerability Exposure Database (DevLIVE+) was developed as an expansion of the Climate and Disaster Exposure Database (ClimEx.db) from the UNDP, Australian Government, and Climate Change Commission’s Resilience and Preparedness toward Inclusive Development (RAPID) program which is focused as an exposure database.TheDevLIVE+, one of the innovations of the UNDP, was created to assist the local government units (LGUs) in collecting, organizing, visualizing, and managing their local data and information. This would aid decision- makers in making evidence-based and data-driven decisions to address local issues better and facilitate service delivery. Moreover, this platform would support the LGUs in assessing vulnerabilities and exposures to hazards and risks, and in creating development pathways toward sustainable and progressive communities. Further, this will enable the local executives to make cost-effective and smart decisions in a timely manner.With the rollout of the DevLIVE+, with the inclusion of the Community-based Monitoring System (CBMS) forms, in the Municipal Government of Talibon, Bohol, their localized database will be made available, and competencies in data collection and management of data managers will be enhanced. This will ensure that administrative policies and decisions of their local chief executive for more inclusive development, poverty reduction, disaster risk reduction, and crises prevention and management are guided by local evidence-based vulnerability and exposure data and information. This initiative is expected for the Municipality of Talibon, Bohol, to promote and ultimately contribute towards achieving the SDGs.The project initiation plan will work towards the above-mentioned outcome, through the achievement of the following output:Output 1: Local government unit (LGU) has access to information and communication technology and inclusive digital solutions targeting the most vulnerable.

title.narrative: Managing Recovery from Disasters in the Philippines

description.narrative: The Philippines is considered one of the most disaster-prone countries in the world. Due to its geographical location, both along the typhoon belt and the Pacific Ring of Fire, the country ranks among the top 10 countries most vulnerable to climate-induced geohazards. With the climate and disaster risks and vulnerabilities of the country, there is an urgent need for timely, well-planned, gender-sensitive inclusive intervention. The UNDP Philippines Country Office (CO) is implementing its Managing Recovery from Disasters in the Philippines Project Initiation Plan (MRDP PIP) which allows it to deliver high-impact recovery and resilience strategies and programs in the context of a climate change-induced new normal. The project aims to strengthen the capacity of the Philippine government, both at the national and local levels, and partner communities in facilitating the timely and sustainable recovery of affected families devastated by disasters. The MRDP PIP will strengthen disaster recovery capabilities of UNDP

title.narrative: Accelerating NDC through Circular Economy in Cities

description.narrative: Waste, including marine litter, is one of the most pressing issues in the Philippines and a significant contributor togreenhouse gas emissions There is recognition that this complex challenge requires a systemic approach and a shift to a circular economic model.The project aims to support the Nationally Determined Contribution through enabling and accelerating the country’stransition to circular economy, with demonstration in key cities. By doing so, it will help the country achieve economic, social and environmental goals, while also grasping the potential for substantial and accelerated reductions in GHG emissions across all sectors, while also supporting recovery from the pandemic.This will be delivered through three main outputs, in alignment with UNDP’s Climate Promise:1. Data-driven and gender responsive analytical systems and policies developed to support the transition to acircular economy in support of NDC.2. title.narrative: I-LEAD Project

description.narrative: The objective of the NZ MFAT Design Phase is to develop the project concept into a PIF: Indigenous Women and Girls Leadership and Empowerment in Ancestral Domains (I-LEAD) Project. During this Design Phase, recommendations on the key activities to be undertaken during the Inception Phase will also be crafted to encapsulate the project preparation assessments and studies that can strengthen the formulation of the Project Document (ProDoc).Potential outcomes from the Project are the following:- Improved education and health outcomes for Indigenous children and women, leading to the improvement of intergenerational well- being of Indigenous peoples in Mindanao.- Increased participation of Indigenous women in social, economic and political decision making, leading to greater social cohesion and inter-generational change for women and girls in the Mindanao region.

title.narrative: Activity web page
EU-GEPP (LGU Component)

description.narrative: The Project aims to enhance the capacities and practices of local government units (LGUs) on circular waste economy in collaboration with private and civil society organizations (CSOs), with focus on youth and gender equality. Key results expected at the end of the Project include:• Reduced volume of waste generated and sent to landfills in partner LGUs • Greenhouse gas emissions reduced and avoided (tonnes of CO2eq) from reduced waste and implementation of circular economy initiatives• At least 20 LGUs have established circular economy models, including introduction of source separation and collection systems for wastes recycling, diversion, repurposing and other CE initiatives• Additional number of organizations applying CE and sustainable consumption and production practices in partner LGUs• Additional jobs (including green jobs) created across the circular economy value chain, disaggregated by gender and groups• At least 10 LGUs in which nationally defined green procurement criteria have been applied• About 15 LGUs that feed their NDC reporting with improved gender and youth sensitive data on co-benefits of CEThe Project is part of the larger European Union-funded Green Economy Programme in the Philippines, which supports the Philippines’ transition towards a green economy, including circular economy, reducing waste and plastics, as well as to increase material and resource efficiency and renewable energy deployment to support climate change mitigation. The transition to circular economy brings ecological and economic benefits which can positively impact businesses, governments, informal sector, and civil society by reducing use of raw materials, creating economic opportunities from waste materials, and generation of minimal to no pollution.

title.narrative: PBF Contributing to Sustainable Peace in the BARMM by Supporting IDP

description.narrative: This project aims to address the causes and impacts of conflict-induced displacement in BARMM; contribute to conditions conducive for longer-term solutions, especially peace and security, access to rights and services, socio-economic inclusion and strengthened governance processes; and create a safe, voluntary, and dignified pathway for IDPs to achieve tailored solutions to resolve their displacement. It will do so by (1) empowering the IDP communities and relevant institutions at local and regional levels to establish and drive conducive policies, programming, planning and process for locally led and defined solutions to displacement and, (2) strengthening the peace mechanisms at regional and local levels to capacity and resolve to prevent and transform conflicts leading to displacements in the project’s locations, which will be guided by data collected under the project in order to support the locally-led and defined solutions to displacements. The project will maximize the relationships between the RUNOs and the BARMM, LGUs, private sector and civil society organizations partners, particularly those with presence, operational capacity and experience in the target communities. The project will be implemented at a critical period in the lead-up to the BARMM elections in May 2025. As the 2025 elections will end the current transition and determine the future regional balance of power, conflict and political dynamics are evolving rapidly, and in this context the unresolved problems of displacement - as the result of both horizontal and vertical conflicts - can lead to further instability and undermine the ongoing peacebuilding efforts. Meanwhile, IDP returns, local integration, or relocation are not sustainable without the requisite conditions including safety, security, and socio-economic development. Supporting the major steps to resolving displacements and instituting a stronger foundation to prevent displacements will be an important component for achieving a

title.narrative: National Plastic Action Partnership in the Philippines

description.narrative: Plastic pollution is recognized as a symptom of deeper systemic issues, including unsustainable production and consumption patterns, inadequate waste management infrastructure, legal frameworks, and enforcement, as well as economic, behavioral, and political factors. There is a pressing need for holistic solutions that address these root causes comprehensively.The National Plastic Action Partnership (NPAP) is a collaborative initiative spearheaded by the World Economic Forum (WEF) and the Global Plastic Action Partnership (GPAP). Its aim is to tackle the global challenge of plastic pollution by bringing together stakeholders from various sectors to develop and implement strategies that reduce plastic pollution and transition towards a circular economy. This can be achieved through 1) establishing organizational and operation procedures of NPAP and developing stakeholder engagement mechanism, 2) generate evidences from studies that feed into national action roadmap and community engagement, and 3) strengthening coordination and collaborations for initiatives on plastic action.UNDP Philippines will act as the NPAP Secretariat for the Philippines. The NPAP Secretariat in the Philippines will play a critical role in coordinating and implementing the NPAPs initiatives in the country.

In addition, this grant supports countries in preparing and submitting their 7th National Report on the implementation of the Convention on Biological Diversity and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.

title.narrative: PROSPER Bangsamoro-UNDP PHL

description.narrative: The Bangsamoro peace process is at a critical stage as it approaches the end of its transition period in 2025. The interim Bangsamoro Government is focused on delivering key initiatives, but urgent attention is needed for marginalized and vulnerable communities. The Programme on Stabilization, Peace and Resilience in the Bangsamoro (PROSPER-Bangsamoro) aims to:- Facilitate technical and operational support to BARMM ministries, offices and agencies (MILG, MPOS, PSRO) and local government units in enhancing institutional capacities and convergence for inclusive and responsive governance;- Enable conditions for stabilization, social cohesion and community resilience through assistance for meaningful inclusionand participation of women, youth, Indigenous Peoples, civil society organizations and local social formations in conflictpreventionand peacebuilding; and,- Accompany normalization processes through support for peace process mechanisms and for the transition and transformation of former combatants and their communities towards peaceful and productive civilian lives and Moro revolutionary groups into social movements implementing PROSPER-Bangsamoro,PROSPER-Bangsamoro will collaborate with regional and local governments, civil society, and marginalized groups, building on existing networks established by previous peacebuilding programs. It aims to empower various community representatives, including women peacebuilders and former combatants.

title.narrative: PPG GBFF Philippines Biodiversity Financing Programme

description.narrative: The objective of the GBFF PPG is to develop the project concept into a full project: Philippines Biodiversity Financing Program. As described in the project concept (PPG Request), this project aims to increase public and private sector biodiversity/nature-positive investments through the implementation of the National Biodiversity Finance Plan to support the accelerated achievement of the Kunming Montreal-Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF) aligned Philippine Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (PBSAP) targets. The final outputs of the GEF PPG are:1 UNDP-GEF Project Document (ProDoc).2 Mandatory annexes to the ProDoc ; 3 GBFF CEO Endorsement Request and4 Validation Workshop report (This is mandatory for moderate, substantial and high risk projects)

title.narrative: PPG Green and Resilient Cities in the Philippines

description.narrative: The objective of the GEF PPG is to develop the project concept into a full project: Green and Resilient Cities in the Philippines. This project aims to advance integrated and systems-based approaches towards enhanced biodiversity, nature-based and low carbon approaches to urban development in six key cities in the Philippines (Baguio, Caloocan, Cotabato, Cebu, Davao, Quezon). The project will focus on four distinct outcomes, which include: (1) Strengthened capacities for integrated approaches for green, biodiverse and climate resilient urban development; (2) Expansion of green spaces, improved biodiversity protection, reduced GHG emissions, improved air and water quality and waste management; (3) Increased financing for green, nature positive and climate investments; and (4) Capacities of other cities enhanced through targeted technical assistance and knowledge exchange. The project proposal will build on relevant and valuable initiatives developed by the DENR as well as lessons learned, and experiences on other successfully implemented GEF Projects, including DREAMS and LCT.

title.narrative: Advancing WPS Phase IV & V

description.narrative: The project aims to enhance womens and youths meaningful participation in peacebuilding in Bangsamoro by strengthening institutional and community capacities. It focuses on implementing the Women, Peace, and Security (WPS) agenda, supporting women mediators, former combatants, and women affected by extremism. The project provides technical support, promotes peace mediation, and assists in establishing womens committees and education programs. It also integrates UNSCR 1325 resolutions into regional policies, advocating for inclusive governance. Key areas include conflict prevention, early warning, reintegration of combatants, and empowering women-led initiatives for long-lasting peace and stability in Bangsamoro.

title.narrative: Project Rebuild: Support for the MILF Amnesty Process

description.narrative: The Project aims to contribute to addressing the root causes of conflict and violence in the Bangsamoro region by providing a pathway for individuals who have been involved in armed struggle to transition and transform into peaceful and productive members of the community. The proposed Project will seek to deliver the following outputs: (1) Provision of technical and operational support for the National Amnesty Commission and Local Amnesty Boards; and, (2) Provision of needs-based support for amnesty applicants and their families in completing the processes and requirements needed for amnesty applications, including legal and documentation missions for MILF amnesty applicants.The above initiatives are expected to contribute to the broader goal of achieving lasting stability and development in the BARMM and in the Philippines.

title.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative - Phase II (BIOFIN Philippines)

description.narrative: While the estimated cost of the Philippine Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (PBSAP) implementation within the given time frame of 2015-2028 is PhP24B/year, a public and private expenditure review revealed that current government (including local governments) spending on biodiversity is only PhP5B/year which leaves a financing gap of PhP19B/year to implement the PBSAP fully. Gross domestic product (GDP) measured at constant prices from 2008-13 averaged PhP5 trillion/year. Measured against this metric, the estimated biodiversity spending of all agencies (including Official Development Assistance) is a measly 0.08% of GDP for this period. The country’s national budget stood at PhP1.6 trillion for the same period and biodiversity spending represents only 0.31% of the national budget. Biodiversity spending can be compared to the share of the agricultural, fish, and forestry sector contribution to the GDP, which is a basic sector that depends on biodiversity. This sector’s contribution to GDP from 2008 to 2013 is 11.0% while the fishery sector alone contributes 2.5%. Thus, income derived from the fishery sector is roughly 31 times the current biodiversity spending. With the completion of the policy and institutional review, expenditure review and finance needs assessment, a biodiversity finance plan was drafted in 2017 and its financial targets were vetted by the Project Steering Committee. Several finance solutions covering the following areas are being implemented:• Improving biodiversity expenditure reporting at the national level• Mainstreaming biodiversity in DENR regional programs• Finance solutions involving the citizenry (crowdfunding, diaspora, mobile game application)• Mainstreaming biodiversity in local government units (fee systems, accessing ER 1-94)• Investment program for protected areas• Private sector engagement (corporate social responsibility, public-private partnership)• Support to studies on biodiversity financing

title.narrative: Project For Raising Voter's Awareness and Promoting Digitalization of the Electoral Process in Bangsamoro

description.narrative: In May 2025, the Philippines will hold national and Bangsamoro Parliament elections in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM). These elections are key for promoting democratic governance and inclusion, but challenges such as voter disenfranchisement, particularly among women, youth, indigenous people, and a lack of awareness about the Parliaments role, persist. To address these issues, UNDP, with COMELEC, and the Embassy of Japan, will focus on improving voter education, utilizing data analytics to support vulnerable groups, and digitalizing election processes to ensure timely and accessible information. The initiative aims to enhance electoral participation, data literacy, and information accessibility.

title.narrative: Inclusive, Competitive and Responsible Digital Philippines - Digital-PINAS

description.narrative: The Inclusive, Competitive and Responsible Digital Philippines (Digital PINAS) aims to leverage digital innovation for inclusive growth and resilience, aligning with UN frameworks and SDGs. It addresses digital disparities among marginalized groups through a three-pillar strategy: enhancing MSME capabilities, improving digital access, and fostering gender-responsive policies. Expected outcomes by 2027 include enhanced MSME digital capabilities, improved access to digital services for entrepreneurs, and gender responsive regulatory environment.

title.narrative: Global Accelerator for Jobs and Social Protection

description.narrative: The UN Joint Programme (JP) on “Supporting the acceleration of decent job creation and enhancing social protection for just transition in the Philippines” aims to accelerate the implementation of the countrys national policy and programme frameworks through the development of innovative and responsive strategies for decent jobs creation, accessible and integrated social protection, and just transition. Two major outcomes are envisioned: (1) the adoption of a Global Accelerator Roadmap through national, sub-national, and sectoral dialogues and key consultations with stakeholders; and (2) enhanced capacities of institutions and relevant stakeholders to fully implement the GA Roadmap. Specifically, the JP will support the Philippine Development Plan 2023-2028, the Philippine Labor and Employment Plan, and the Social Protection Plan. It will contribute to the long-term National Employment Master Plan as mandated by the new Trabaho Para sa Bayan (TPB) Act and will enhance capacities of multi-stakeholders to participate in the multi-level consultations on the GA Roadmap formulation to be steered by the TPB Inter- Agency Committee. As earlier defined by the Philippines, the GA Roadmap will focus on two sectors identified among key employment generators – the construction and transportation sectors. The JP brings together critical national and sectoral stakeholders, leveraging the resources of the UN strategically led by the International Labour Organization, United Nations Development Programme, and the United Nations Children’s Fund, and key government agencies particularly the Department of Labor and Employment, National Economic and Development Agency, Department of Trade and Industry, Department of Transportation, and Department of Social Welfare and Development.

title.narrative: PHL-TCCF Plastics Circularity

description.narrative: The Management of Plastics Waste and Circular Interventions on Plastics in South Southeast Asia and the Pacific project aims to address the critical issue of plastic waste management in nine countries across South and Southeast Asia and the Pacific. This initiative targets countries with varying levels of waste management infrastructure and plastic waste challenges, including India, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, Maldives, Sri Lanka, Philippines, Cambodia, and Vietnam. The project is designed to support the transition to a circular economy by improving plastic waste management, promoting recycling, and reducing plastic leakage into the environment. By focusing on these targeted areas, the project will contribute to better public health, environmental protection, and the development of sustainable waste management practices. The project is expected to deliver the following key outputs across participating countries; contributing to collect at least 53,930 tons of plastics over the project implementation and support 69,600 direct beneficiaries.

title.narrative: (BAEP) Modeling Livelihood Solutions for Coastal Communities in Basilan

description.narrative: The project seeks to contribute towards supporting the fisheries sector in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region inMuslim Mindanao (BARMM), specifically within the province of Basilan, by modelling community-level livelihoodsolutions for coastal communities by empowering participating fisherfolk communities and supporting an enablingenvironment for sustainable fishery livelihoods. It is designed to respond to the need to accelerate socioeconomicrecovery programs, sustain the region’s progress towards poverty reduction, and ensure economic growth that isinclusive to all Bangsamoro peoples. Furthermore, through the provision of support for economic development andresilience building against multiple risks, UNDP seeks to contribute to durable peace and sustainable development inthe region.

title.narrative: Support to Philippine NDC (NDC Support)

description.narrative: The Support to Philippine Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) Project assists the Government ofthe Philippines (GoP) in advancing its climate goals by establishing net-zero and climate-resilientdevelopment pathways aligned with the Paris Agreement. The project supports the enhancement of thecountry’s NDC through a five-step process: scoping, baseline updates, sector assessments, cross-cuttinganalyses, and drafting NDC revisions. Key outputs include input documents for the NDC 3.0 in the wasteand industrial processes and product use (IPPU) sectors; recommendations on integrating Nature-basedSolutions (NbS) and Circular Economy (CE) principles; and strengthen gender-responsiveness andstakeholder engagement in updating the NDC. Guided by the Climate Change Commission, DENR, andNDC Technical Working Group, the project aligns with the Philippines development priorities andinternational climate commitments, fostering equitable, evidence-based climate solutions.

title.narrative: I-LEAD (Indigenous Women and Girls - Leadership and Empowerment in Ancestral Domains)

description.narrative: The Indigenous Women and Girls - Leadership and Empowerment in Ancestral Domains (I-LEAD) Project will be implemented through three linked components: (i) Strengthening Ancestral Domain Governance with Human Rights and Gender Perspectives; (ii) Promoting Gender-Equitable and Culturally Appropriate Economic Empowerment; and (iii) Enhancing the Capacity of the NCIP and other Stakeholders on Human Rights-Based, Gender Responsive, and Culturally Appropriate Policy Development and Knowledge Management. Together, these components will deliver nine (9) key outputs that contribute to three long-term outcomes:(i) Resilient, Responsive, and Relevant indigenous women and girls, and ICCs/IPs; (ii) Economically empowered indigenous women and girls; and (iii) Improved access to basic services by indigenous women and girls. Collectively, these advance the project’s overarching goal: ‘ICCs/IPs in the Philippines are economically empowered and resilient, enjoying decent and sustainable livelihoods that support their cultural integrity and enhanced intergenerational well-being’. Implemented over four years (2026–2030), I-LEAD adopts an integrated, rights-based, gender-transformative, and systems-strengthening approach, convening ICCs/IPs, local government units, national government agencies, and civil society to co-create solutions anchored in indigenous rights, cultural identity, human rights, and gender equality. The project emphasizes evidence-based planning, participatory decision-making, capacity development, digital connection, and scalable women and youth-led enterprise models to advance inclusive development.

title.narrative: PHL JSB RE3ACHS Lanao Del Sur

description.narrative: The project aims to accelerate the adoption of clean energy, energy efficiency, and just energy transition in the province of Lanao del Sur (LDS) in support of the Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) of the Philippines, the National Renewable Energy Program (NREP) 2020-2040 and as a contribution to peace and development in the region following the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region for Muslim Mindanao (BARMM) 12-point Priority Agenda. It will support the expansion of renewable energy facilities in the provincial hospital and commission three (3) additional public health facilities units to ensure access to reliable, affordable energy supply. The project will serve the needs of an average of 40,000 households annually. LDS represents at least 30% of the population in BARMM. Despite hosting the major power plants in the region, it remains energy-poor with power voltage dropping from 220V to 100V. This power drop has damaged hospital equipment and disturbs medical services, especially during climate and conflict-induced calamities.Scaling up initiatives in the area and facilitating the preparation of local renewable energy (RE) and energy efficiency (EE) plans and projects will demonstrate the viability of RE and EE solutions at broader or regional levels, leading to increased public and private sector investments in the sector.

title.narrative: PIMS-10242 GEF SGP 8th Operational Phase (Part 1) PHILIPPINES

description.narrative: SGP-OP8 builds on 30 years of successful experience in empowering local CSOs and CBOs in community driven initiatives that have enhanced wellbeing, increased awareness and resilience, and generated global environmental benefits. With economic development pressures intensifying in the developing world and the associated rising inequalities, local communities, particularly vulnerable and disadvantaged groups are becoming increasingly marginalized. The objective of Part 1 of SGP OP8 is to engage local CSOs/CBOs in landscape-seascape approaches across the 99 participating countries, providing them access to knowledge and information, capacitating them through learning-by-doing, skills development, and delivering technical and grant assistance for interventions that enhance socioeconomic conditions and generate global environmental benefits. In line with the GEF-8 SGP Implementation Arrangements Paper, core SGP resources are to be allocated equally among all eligible countries.The proposed strategy, elaborated through four complementary outcomes, reflects the key features of GEF Small Grants Programme 2.0, including new approaches to support youth, women and Indigenous Peoples, linking up with complementary partnerships, cooperating with the two additional GEF agencies, and leveraging opportunities with other GEF programs. SGP-OP8 will facilitate opportunities for innovation and scaling up, catalyze multi-stakeholder alliances, and leverage dialogue platforms for greater impact. An important comparative advantage of SGP is the focus on social inclusion and vulnerable groups, including women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, the elderly and youth. Expected results under OP8-Part 1 include 225,000 ha of land and ecosystems under restoration, 3,800,000 ha of landscapes under improved practices, 90,000 ha of marine protected areas under improved management, 270,000 ha of marine habitat under improved practices, and 500,000 direct beneficiaries, of whom 250,000

title.narrative: EU-PH Partnership in Reinforcing Capacity, Transparency, and Efficiency in Public Procurement

description.narrative: Public procurement in the Philippines remains a strategic area for governance reform. Despite ongoing digitalization and regulatory improvements, the system continues to face persistent challenges related to transparency, accountability, and citizen trust. High-profile procurement controversies have hinted at systemic vulnerabilities in procurement oversight, data transparency, and stakeholder participation. The passage of the New Government Procurement Act (NGPA) in 2024 represents a landmark effort to reverse this trajectory. However, the success of this legal transition depends on strengthening the capacity of oversight institutions, enhancing access to and use of procurement data, and ensuring that civil society can play an active role in monitoring and shaping procurement practices.The Project responds to these challenges by supporting the operationalization of the NGPA through the interconnected strategies of strengthening institutional capacities for oversight and policy development, improving procurement data systems and governance; and enabling civil society organizations to monitor, audit, and advocate for reforms. Expected results include improved capacity of COA and CSOs to conduct data-driven participatory audits; evidence-based and inclusive policy recommendations produced by GPPB; functional, OCDS-compliant dashboards managed by PS-DBM and PS-PhilGEPS; and institutionalized training and SOPs that embed transparency, accountability, and gender responsiveness into procurement operations. Together, these efforts will contribute to a more transparent, inclusive, and accountable public procurement system in the Philippines, aligned with international standards and national development goals.

title.narrative: PHL EU4Normalisation

description.narrative: The Project seeks to: (a) enhance capacities of national and local institutions to plan, implement, and manage normalisation interventions; and, (b) support the transformation of MILF camps and their communities to peaceful and productive areas through inclusive and sustainable development interventions. Key components include: (1) capacity building for improving development coordination and planning, information dissemination and management capacities of duty bearers and claimholders; (2) enhancing and sustaining camp transformation initiatives through increasing access to communal facilities, and improving capacities of peoples’ organizations to manage them; and, (3) livelihood support for increasing opportunities among target population groups, including the marginalized and vulnerable ones, to improve their socio-economic conditions.

title.narrative: EU-Partnership for Peace and Development in Mindanao (PPDM): Good Governance in BARMM

description.narrative: The Joint Programme (JP) under the Partnership for Peace and Development in Mindanao (PPDM) supports the consolidation of peace, good governance, and access to justice in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM). Anchored in the UN Cooperation Framework (CF) outcome on good governance and aligned with BARMM’s Priority Agenda and the Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP), the JP responds to the urgent need to institutionalize accountable governance systems and operationalize an inclusive, culturally responsive justice architecture in a post-conflict and transition context.Peace and development in BARMM are mutually reinforcing and depend on deliberate, inclusive, and institutionalized reforms. Despite important peace gains, governance and justice-related drivers of conflict persist, including weak public financial management (PFM), fragmented planning and budgeting systems, uneven service delivery, and limited access to justice—particularly for women, indigenous peoples, internally displaced persons (IDPs), youth, and other marginalized groups. The JP addresses these structural challenges by strengthening institutional capacities, embedding gender-responsive and conflict-sensitive approaches, and supporting the operationalization of the tri-justice system (formal, Shari’ah, and tribal). These reforms are essential to sustaining peace dividends, strengthening public trust, and ensuring the legitimacy and resilience of BARMM institutions during a critical transition period.The JP contributes directly to CF outcomes on accountable governance, rule of law, and social cohesion, and advances SDG 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities), SDG 13 (Climate Action), and SDG 17 (Partnerships). Primary beneficiaries include key BARMM institutions.The JP is implemented by UNDP and UNOPS, leveraging their complementary strengths.

title.narrative: PIMS-10333_PHL_EA BBNJ

description.narrative: The objective of this grant is to support countries in: (a) assessing the extent to which their current legal, policy and institutional framework allows for promptly becoming parties to the Agreement under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ Agreement) and enables their implementation of the Agreement; (b) carrying out preparatory work in the context of their ratification, approval, acceptance or accession process towards becoming parties to the BBNJ Agreement; and (c) supporting their readiness to implement the BBNJ Agreement.

title.narrative: PIMS-10351 PPG EBC in AD

description.narrative: This project aims to address the economic drivers of biodiversity loss in ancestral domains by making traditional stewardship economically competitive with alternative employment, demonstrating how a combination of government integration, market-based revenue streams, and risk-reduction tools can reverse degradation while generating replicable models across the Philippines’ 1,531 ancestral domains covering 13.2 million hectares. It embeds economic viability into conservation planning and regulatory processes so that NCIP and partners can scale results beyond pilot sites and sustain them over time.
